content:
# Carbonation

**Summary**: The chemical reaction between CO2 and minerals in cement, leading to the formation of calcium carbonate.

---

## How It Appears in the Thesis Collection

- [[2020-mahmoud|Mahmoud, 2020]] studied the carbonation process of Class G cement under high-pressure and high-temperature conditions.

- [[2024-badhafere|BADHAFERE, 2024]] analyzed the mineral trapping of CO2 through carbonate formation.

- [[2025-alhubail|Alhubail, 2025]] studied the carbonation process in mudrocks with varying organic richness.

- [[2025-alghawi|Alghawi, 2025]] investigated the carbonation of basaltic rocks under various environmental conditions.

## Scientific Context

- [[2020-mahmoud|Mahmoud, 2020]] Critical for assessing the durability of well cements in carbon capture projects.

- [[2024-badhafere|BADHAFERE, 2024]] analyzed the mineral trapping of CO2 through carbonate formation.

- [[2025-alhubail|Alhubail, 2025]] studied the carbonation process in mudrocks with varying organic richness.

- [[2025-alghawi|Alghawi, 2025]] investigated the carbonation of basaltic rocks under various environmental conditions.

## Measurement and Characterization Methods

- [[2020-mahmoud|Mahmoud, 2020]] Measured as depth (mm) and rate using CT scans and microscopy.

## Key Findings Across Sources

- Carbonation leads to the dissolution of portlandite (CH) and conversion of C-S-H into amorphous silica gel, increasing porosity. ([[2020-mahmoud|Mahmoud, 2020, p. 36]])


- The precipitation of carbonate minerals is a significant aspect of CO2 sequestration as it can contribute to the long-term immobilization of CO2. ([[2024-badhafere|BADHAFERE, 2024, p. 31]])

- Carbonate mudrocks undergo significant mineralogical changes during interaction with CO2. ([[2025-alhubail|Alhubail, 2025, p. 10]])

- Optimal conditions for carbonation included high pressure, low pH, and magnesium-rich rock. ([[2025-alghawi|Alghawi, 2025, p. 16]])
